entries.meaning: The science of posing and answering data questions related to business.
entries.scenario: A company wants to know not just what its sales numbers say, but why they matter and what to do next - turning that question into a rigorous data investigation.
entries.label: Analytics

entries.meaning: The type of analytics that summarizes and reports historical data - answering 'what happened?'
entries.scenario: A retailer combines sales and loyalty data just to see which products each customer segment already prefers.
entries.label: Descriptive analytics

entries.meaning: The type of analytics that uses historical patterns to forecast future outcomes - answering 'what will happen?'
entries.scenario: A firm uses past sales patterns to forecast demand for next quarter.
entries.label: Predictive analytics

entries.meaning: The type of analytics that recommends the best action given goals and constraints - answering 'what should we do?'
entries.scenario: An airline doesn't just predict demand - it uses optimization to decide exactly which dynamic price to set for every seat.
entries.label: Prescriptive analytics

entries.meaning: Reasoning from samples to conclusions under uncertainty - one of the four core tool areas analysts draw on.
entries.scenario: An analyst infers what an entire customer base thinks from a survey of only 500 people.
entries.label: Statistics

entries.meaning: Collecting data from a representative subset of a population, allowing inference with some sampling error.
entries.scenario: Instead of contacting every single customer, a company polls a representative sample of 1,000 and infers what the rest likely think.
entries.label: a Survey

entries.meaning: Collecting data from every single unit in a population - accurate, but costly and time-consuming.
entries.scenario: A government counts every single citizen in the country rather than a sample of them.
entries.label: a Census

entries.meaning: Data that is organized into clear fields, like transaction records or spreadsheets.
entries.scenario: A sales log with a clean row for every transaction and a column for each field is easy for software to query directly.
entries.label: Structured data

entries.meaning: To extract data from sources like databases, APIs, or websites - a key step in the data collection process.
entries.scenario: An analyst writes a script to scrape data from an online platform and pull records from an API.
entries.label: Data Mining

entries.meaning: One of the four Vs of big data - the sheer quantity of data generated.
entries.scenario: A social network generates billions of posts every single day - far too much for any one machine to handle.
entries.label: Volume, one of the four Vs of big data

entries.meaning: One of the four Vs of big data - the speed at which data arrives.
entries.scenario: A stock exchange's trading data streams in thousands of updates per second, faster than any single system could process one at a time.
entries.label: Velocity, one of the four Vs of big data

entries.meaning: One of the four Vs of big data - the mix of formats data comes in, like text, images, and logs.
entries.scenario: A company's data includes customer reviews, product photos, call-center audio, and web logs, all in totally different formats.
entries.label: Variety, one of the four Vs of big data

entries.meaning: One of the four Vs of big data - the trustworthiness and noise level of the data.
entries.scenario: Some sensor readings in a dataset are clearly broken or contradictory, so an analyst has to judge how much to trust them before using them.
entries.label: Veracity, one of the four Vs of big data

entries.meaning: An open-source framework for storing and processing big data across clusters of computers.
entries.scenario: A company needs to store petabytes of log data across hundreds of cheap machines instead of one expensive server.
entries.label: Hadoop

entries.meaning: A big-data processing engine known for fast, in-memory computation across clusters.
entries.scenario: An analytics team can't afford to wait for slow disk-based processing every time they rerun a big-data job, so they switch to an in-memory cluster engine.
entries.label: Spark

entries.meaning: A data collection method that gathers data from every single unit in a population, such as a census.
entries.scenario: A national census contacts every household in the country rather than a sample of them.
entries.label: Enumeration

entries.meaning: A data collection method where data is generated under controlled conditions to test a hypothesis.
entries.scenario: Researchers test five different fertilizers across different soil types under controlled conditions to see which works best.
entries.label: an Experiment

entries.meaning: Data with no predefined format, like free text, images, audio, or video - harder to analyze directly.
entries.scenario: A dataset of customer service call recordings and product photos can't be queried like a spreadsheet the way clean transaction records can.
entries.label: Unstructured data

entries.meaning: Data where a single unit is tracked over multiple points in time.
entries.scenario: An analyst studies one company's monthly revenue over the past five years.
entries.label: Time-series data

entries.meaning: Data where many different units are each measured at a single point in time.
entries.scenario: An analyst compares 500 different companies' revenue for this quarter only, with no history attached.
entries.label: Cross-sectional data

entries.meaning: Data where multiple units are each tracked over multiple points in time.
entries.scenario: An analyst tracks 500 companies' revenue every quarter for five years straight.
entries.label: Panel data

entries.meaning: A data point or value that looks implausible or wildly different from the rest, worth checking before trusting the data.
entries.scenario: While validating a dataset, an analyst spots a customer record listing an age of -12.
entries.label: an Outlier

entries.meaning: A reliability check confirming that the same measurement, repeated, gives the same result.
entries.scenario: To make sure a satisfaction survey is reliable, a researcher gives the same customer the same questionnaire twice a week apart and checks the answers match.
entries.label: a Test-Retest reliability check

entries.meaning: A model that assigns credit for a conversion across multiple ads or channels, such as first-click or last-click.
entries.scenario: A customer sees a display ad, then a social ad, then clicks a search ad and buys - and marketing needs to decide which ad gets credit for the sale.
entries.label: an Attribution model

entries.meaning: A machine learning approach that learns from labeled examples to predict an outcome.
entries.scenario: A model is trained on thousands of past loan applications explicitly labeled 'defaulted' or 'repaid' so it can predict new applicants.
entries.label: Supervised learning

entries.meaning: A machine learning approach that finds structure - like groups or patterns - in data with no labels at all.
entries.scenario: An algorithm is given raw customer purchase data with no labels and asked to find natural groupings on its own.
entries.label: Unsupervised learning

entries.meaning: A data scale that categorizes data with no inherent order, such as colors or categories.
scenarios: A dataset labels each product's color as green, yellow, or red - there's no sense in which one color 'ranks higher' than another.
Every employee is assigned an ID number purely to tell them apart in the system - the numbers carry no ranking or magnitude at all.
A customer database records blood type as A, B, AB, or O - just labels, with no type being 'more' than another.
Zip codes look numeric, but 90210 isn't 'more' than 10001 in any meaningful sense - they're just location labels.
Marital status is recorded as single, married, divorced, or widowed - categories with no inherent order among them.
Jersey numbers on a sports team identify players, but number 23 isn't 'twice as good' as number 11, or even 'more' than it in any real sense.
A restaurant directory tags each entry by cuisine type - Italian, Chinese, Mexican - purely to categorize, not to rank one above another.
A survey records gender as a set of categories with no built-in ranking between them.
License plate numbers uniquely identify vehicles, but plate 'DEF456' isn't greater than 'ABC123' in any sense that matters.
A company's departments are labeled Sales, Marketing, and IT - useful for grouping data, but none ranks above the others.
Airport codes like JFK, LAX, and ORD are just labels for locations, with no order or magnitude implied.
A yes/no survey question sorts respondents into two categories with no ranking between 'yes' and 'no' themselves.
Social security numbers uniquely identify people, but you'd never average two of them or say one is 'bigger' in any meaningful sense.
entries.label: the Nominal scale

entries.meaning: A data scale with a meaningful order, but where the gaps between categories aren't uniformly measurable.
scenarios: A survey ranks customer education as Illiterate, Primary, or Graduate - clearly ordered, but the 'distance' between levels isn't a fixed, measurable amount.
Patient health status is tracked as Sick, Recovering, or Healthy - a clear progression, but with no fixed 'unit' separating each stage.
A company classifies customer income as Low, Medium, or High - ordered categories, but the gap between Low and Medium isn't necessarily the same as between Medium and High.
Military ranks go from Private to Sergeant to Captain - clearly ordered, but the seniority gap between adjacent ranks isn't a fixed, equal unit.
In a race, runners finish 1st, 2nd, and 3rd - the order is clear, but the actual time gap between 1st and 2nd could be totally different from the gap between 2nd and 3rd.
Boxers are grouped into weight classes like Flyweight, Featherweight, and Heavyweight - clearly ordered by weight, but the classes aren't evenly spaced.
University students are classified as Freshman, Sophomore, Junior, or Senior - an ordered progression with no fixed numeric gap between stages.
In a taste test, panelists rank three products as their 1st, 2nd, and 3rd choice - clear order, but no measurable 'distance' between the rankings.
Pain is described in words as None, Mild, Moderate, or Severe - clearly ordered, but 'Moderate' isn't necessarily an equal step above 'Mild' the way a number would be.
Hotel quality is classified into star tiers - 3-star, 4-star, 5-star - assigned by inspectors as ordered categories, not as a self-reported numeric score.
Letter grades run A, B, C, D, F - clearly ordered, but the underlying performance gap between an A and a B isn't guaranteed to equal the gap between a C and a D.
A graduating class is ranked 1st, 2nd, 3rd, all the way to 200th - a clear order, but the gap in performance between consecutive ranks is not a fixed amount.
A survey lists agreement as Strongly Disagree, Disagree, Neutral, Agree, Strongly Agree, presented as labeled categories rather than assigned numbers.
entries.label: the Ordinal scale

entries.meaning: Drawing conclusions only from the survivors of a selection process, while the missing cases are excluded.
entries.scenario: Engineers plan to armor a bomber's wings, where returning planes show the most bullet holes - but planes hit in the engines never made it back to be counted.
entries.label: Survivorship Bias

entries.meaning: An error where the sampling frame used to collect data misses part of the population it's meant to represent.
entries.scenario: A phone survey about internet habits accidentally excludes everyone who only owns a mobile phone with no landline.
entries.label: Coverage Error

entries.meaning: To confirm a data finding by checking it against a second, independent source.
entries.scenario: Before trusting a survey result, a researcher also checks it against government statistics and a completely separate dataset.
entries.label: Triangulation

entries.meaning: A machine learning approach that learns through trial, reward, and feedback over time, rather than labels.
entries.scenario: A system tries different actions, gets a reward signal for good outcomes and a penalty for bad ones, and gradually learns the best policy through repeated trial and error.
entries.label: Reinforcement learning

entries.meaning: Data-driven decision making as a repeating cycle, not a single one-off report.
entries.scenario: After Wald's recommendation saved bomber crews, that same decision fed straight into the next question the military needed answered.
entries.label: the analytics Loop, a repeating cycle

entries.meaning: Research done when the problem itself isn't even clear yet - so-called 'unknown-unknowns.'
entries.scenario: A country head only knows 'sales are down' but has no idea why - is it the product, the brand, or the price? Identifying the real problem is itself the problem here.
entries.label: Exploratory research

entries.meaning: Research that tests whether one specific action produces one specific effect.
entries.scenario: A manager doesn't just want to know who buys the product - she wants to know whether cutting the price would actually raise sales.
entries.label: Causal research

entries.meaning: A data quality problem where respondents misremember details or give socially desirable rather than accurate answers.
entries.scenario: Survey respondents asked about their spending three months ago tend to round numbers and remember themselves more favorably than the receipts would show.
entries.label: Recall Bias

entries.meaning: A data quality problem where people contacted for data simply don't respond, skewing the sample toward those who do.
entries.scenario: Only the most satisfied (or most furious) customers bother to fill out the feedback survey, while everyone in between stays silent.
entries.label: Non-response Bias

entries.meaning: A data scale with equal, measurable gaps between values, but no true zero - differences are meaningful, ratios aren't.
scenarios: 20 degrees isn't 'twice as hot' as 10 degrees, even though the numbers say so - because zero doesn't mean 'no temperature at all.'
An IQ score of 150 isn't 'twice as intelligent' as a score of 75 - there's no such thing as an IQ of zero meaning 'no intelligence at all.'
The year 2000 isn't 'twice as much time' as the year 1000 - there's no 'year zero' that means the complete absence of time.
On a 1-to-10 satisfaction rating, an 8 isn't necessarily 'twice as satisfied' as a 4 - the scale has no true zero point of 'zero satisfaction.'
3:00 PM isn't 'twice as late' as 1:30 PM - midnight is just an arbitrary reference point, not the total absence of time.
A standardized test score of 700 (on a 200-800 scale) isn't 'twice as good' as 350 - the scale has no true zero representing a complete absence of ability.
On a 5-point agreement scale (1=Strongly Disagree to 5=Strongly Agree) used numerically, a 4 isn't 'twice as much' agreement as a 2 - there's no true zero point of 'zero opinion.'
A mountain at 'elevation zero' isn't the total absence of height - sea level is just an arbitrary reference point, and land can even go below it.
A solution's pH isn't a ratio scale - pH 0 doesn't mean 'zero acidity,' and pH values can even go negative in extreme cases.
A company's Net Promoter Score can range from -100 to +100, with 0 meaning a neutral balance of promoters and detractors, not 'zero promotion happening.'
A custom 'happiness index' runs from -5 to +5, where 0 means neutral, not 'a complete absence of happiness.'
A golfer's handicap can even go negative for elite players - there's no meaningful zero point representing a total absence of skill.
A statistics class computes z-scores with a mean of exactly 0 - but that 0 means 'average,' not 'a complete absence of the thing being measured.'
entries.label: the Interval scale

entries.meaning: A data scale with equal intervals AND a true zero point, so both differences and ratios are meaningful.
scenarios: A runner who finishes in 10 seconds really is twice as fast as one who finishes in 20 seconds, because 0 seconds genuinely means 'no time elapsed.'
A person weighing 100 kg really is twice as heavy as someone weighing 50 kg, because 0 kg genuinely means 'no weight at all.'
An employee earning $100,000 a year really does earn twice as much as one earning $50,000, since $0 genuinely means 'no income at all.'
A 40-year-old really is twice as old as a 20-year-old, since age 0 genuinely marks the moment of birth - a true starting point.
A store that sold 200 units really did sell twice as many as one that sold 100, since 0 units genuinely means 'nothing sold at all.'
A product priced at $40 really does cost twice as much as one priced at $20, since $0 genuinely means 'free.'
A student who scores 80% on a test really did get twice as many questions right as one who scored 40%, since 0% genuinely means 'no correct answers at all.'
A warehouse that received 0 customer complaints really did receive none at all - and 20 complaints really is twice as many as 10.
A family with 4 children really does have twice as many kids as a family with 2, since 0 children genuinely means childless.
A delivery that traveled 100 km really did go twice as far as one that traveled 50 km, since 0 km genuinely means 'no distance covered at all.'
A webpage that takes 4 seconds to load really is twice as slow as one taking 2 seconds, since 0 seconds genuinely means instantaneous.
An employee with 10 years of experience really has twice as much as one with 5 years, since 0 years genuinely means a brand-new hire.
A city with a population of 2 million really does have twice as many people as one with 1 million, since 0 genuinely means nobody lives there.
entries.label: the Ratio scale
